english: ₒdu-śés-càn ('du shes can) entertaining thoughts of sensual pleasure Glr.; ₒdu-śés slar rnyéd-pa ('du shes slar rnyed pa) to recover from a state of insensibility; as vb.: ₒdu-śés-pa ('du shes pa), mya-ṅan-ₒdás tob du-śés-te (mya ngan 'das thob du shes te) imagining that I shall obtain Nirwāna Thgy. As one of the five púṅ-po (phung po) it is translated by idea (Burn. I, 511), by perception (Köpp. I, 603). The three terms ₒdu-śés-càn ('du shes can), ₒdu-śés-méd-pa ('du shes med pa), ₒdu-śés-med-mín ('du shes med min) may be rendered: having the faculty of thinking, having no faculty of thinking, neither thinking nor not thinking (Dzl. 238, 7), ₒdu-śés-càn ('du shes can) refers to human beings, the two other terms relate to celestial beings (v. Köpp. I, 261, 17 and 26), that are evidently so much the more excellent and exalted, as they are far above all reasoning and thinking. According to another, and (it would seem) more natural interpretation, the first of these three terms implies rational beings (man), the second irrational beings (higher animals), and the third quite irrational creatures (lower animals, worms, reptiles, that are not even possessed of the sensitive powers of the higher animals), whilst the 'long-lived Lhas' of the 17th. heaven are classed together with the common Lhas (who however taken strictly, belong to the 'first world') and on account of their stupidity are believed to be incapable of ever being converted, Thgy.

(eleg. ₒdá-ba ('da' ba), resp. bźúgs-pa (bzhugs pa)) 1. to sit, syn. with sdód-pa (sdod pa); with na (na), la (la) etc.; to sit down with termin. or la (la); to sit up (in bed); ₒdúg-par ₒgyúr ('dug par 'gyur) to get seated Dzl. 78, 6; to remain sitting, to keep one's seat, Dzl. 78, 7; to remain, to stay, ₒdir ma ₒdúg-par soṅ źig ('dir ma 'dug par song zhig) Dzl.; to remain behind, to stay at home, with or without pýir (phyir), kyím-na (khyim na) etc. Dzl. - 2. to be, to exist, to live Glr.: ... skabs-méd ₒdug-go (skabs med 'dug go)! there is no chance of ... Yes, there is! ... ₒdug śés-nas ('dug shes nas) knowing that ... is still alive Dzl.; dráṅ-sroṅ byéd-ciṅ ₒdug (drang srong byed cing 'dug) he lives as a hermit Dzl.; to be, to live at a certain place, gnás-na ₒdúg-pa (gnas na 'dug pa) the being somewhere Gram.; p'a-má gáṅ-na ₒdug (pha ma gang na 'dug) where are my parents now? to be at home Dzl. and elsewh.; to be extant, to be found, caṅ mi ₒdug (cang mi 'dug) nothing is, or was to be found, nothing was there Mil.; as partic. joined with, or put inst. of the possess. pron.: k'o-ráṅ daṅ (k'oi) bu brgyad ₒdúg-pa (kho rang dang (kho'i) bu brgyad 'dug pa) he and his eight children being with him Mil. (yód-pa (yod pa) is construed in the same manner); in quotations: to be found, to be written, to be met with, ... yod zér-ba ... na ₒdug (yod zer ba ... na 'dug) the account of being ... is to be found in ..., Glr. - 3. to be, as copula, in B. often with termin.: kyím-par ₒdúg-pa (khyim par 'dug pa) to be a layman Stg.; rkáṅ-pa krá-bor ₒdug (rkang pa khra bor 'dug) the foot was variously coloured Dzl.; ₒdí-rnams mi-ma-yín-du ₒdúg-pas ('di rnams mi ma yin du 'dug pas) as these are spirits Mil. Generally speaking, this termin. case is not to be pressed, nor always to be explained by: to have become, or to be translated by: in, as in the following: rgya-gár-gyi yí-ger ₒdúg-pas (rgya gar gyi yi ger 'dug pas) to be (written) in the Indian language Glr. - 4. to be, as auxiliar vb., 1. with the termin. of the inf., often merely paraphrastically, e. g. yód-par ₒdúg-pa (yod par 'dug pa) = yód-pa (yod pa) Glr.; frq., however, indicating doubtfulness and uncertainty: ṅa ni saṅ ₒc'í-bar ₒdug (nga ni sang 'chi bar 'dug) may be I shall die to-morrow Glr.; kyed ... yín-par-ₒdug (khyed ... yin par 'dug) you seem to be, you are, I dare say Mil.; ₒgro dgós-par ₒdug ('gro dgos par 'dug) I suppose you must go Glr.; stér-bar ₒdug (ster bar 'dug) it will probably be given Glr.; ma mt'óṅ-na mi rtógs-par ₒdug (ma mthong na mi rtogs par 'dug) if we had not seen it, we should probably not have known it Mil.; in the same manner it is used with yód-pa (yod pa), q.v. - 2. with a verbal root, in ancient lit. hardly ever occurring, in more recent writings used paraphrastically like ₒdúg-pa ('dug pa), with the termin. of the inf. (v. above 1), but not indicating a certain tense, e.g. rdol ₒdug (rdol 'dug) it makes its appearance, comes to light, Glr., bśig ₒdug (bshig 'dug) they were destroyed Glr.; in col. language (in W. at least) it is gen. a sign of the pres. tense: zer ₒdug (zer 'dug) I say, thou sayest etc.; only in Bal. it indicates the fut. tense. - 3. with the gerund in te (te) or nas (nas) vulgo for the pres. or preterite tense, frq.; in B. of so rare occurrence, that it is prob. to be regarded as a vulgarism to be charged on the copyists, and to be cor-
